life_stories.title: I-35W bridge collapse
life_stories.event: Robert was working at a Minneapolis engineering firm when the I-35W bridge collapsed in August 2007. He knew engineers involved in the design. The subsequent investigation found a combination of design flaws, deferred maintenance decisions made by government transportation agencies, and inspection failures. He was not involved — the firm worked on a different set of projects — but the event was close enough to be personal. He spent months reading every technical document released and knew, before the final report, that this was a system failure at the intersection of engineering, government, and budget politics.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Infrastructure and government competence are permanent concerns. Government rates low in trust not from ideology but from direct professional proximity to how deferred public investment decisions create risk. Engineers he respects enormously. Government procurement and maintenance funding decisions he does not.

life_stories.title: Son's medical school admission
life_stories.event: His son spent three years applying to medical school, was rejected twice, was deeply discouraged, and was finally admitted on his third cycle. Robert watched his son navigate a process that seemed simultaneously highly rigorous and highly opaque — where the difference between acceptance and rejection at comparable schools was not always explicable by the candidate's record. His son is now a resident. The system ultimately worked. The path there was harder than it needed to be and Robert lost significant confidence in the admissions process as a meritocratic mechanism.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Trusts scientific and medical professionals at high levels; trusts the institutional access and selection processes that produce them at lower levels. Separates the credential from the credentialing mechanism.

life_stories.title: Employee ownership structure
life_stories.event: Robert's firm transitioned to an employee ownership structure. He was on the committee that designed the share allocation formula. The process was slow, contentious, and periodically unfair in small ways — but it resulted in a firm where he felt genuinely invested in the outcome, not just an employee. He has turned down two higher-paying offers because of this structure.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Employer trust is at the highest end of his scale — not naive, but grounded in a shared governance mechanism he helped build. Business structures with accountability to workers function differently than those without it.

coping_mechanism.description: Reads technical documentation and independent engineering assessments before forming institutional trust judgements. Applies load-bearing analysis to institutional claims.

key_tensions: Professional proximity to government infrastructure failure creates sharper government distrust than most S01 peers
High employer trust is earned through co-ownership structure — not automatic, not general

narrative: When a bridge falls down, it doesn't fall because the engineer didn't know the laws of physics. It falls because a sequence of budget decisions, inspection deferrals, and political priorities made failure statistically inevitable and no single decision-maker was accountable for the cumulative risk. I watched that happen from close range in 2007. That experience shapes everything about how I think about government. I trust engineers — I am one, and I know what rigorous professional practice looks like. I do not trust the procurement systems, the maintenance funding decisions, or the political process that underfunds infrastructure decade after decade. Business I trust more than government for a specific reason: in a competitive market, firms that cut corners in ways that produce failures lose customers or face liability. The correction mechanism works, imperfectly, but it works. Government's correction mechanism — the ballot box — is too slow and too blunt to fix anything structural in a reasonable timeframe. My employer is a special case — I own a piece of it, and that matters. Scientists I rate very highly. CEOs I rate conditionally. Media I read carefully from multiple sources and trust selectively.
decision_bullets: Business ranks first — market correction mechanisms function faster than democratic accountability.
Government trust lowest — deferred infrastructure investment is his paradigmatic case of institutional failure.
Expert credibility (engineers, scientists) very high — professional peer recognition is his primary trust signal.
Employer trust very high — employee-ownership structure creates shared accountability that satisfies his framework.
Media trust low-moderate — consumed in high volume but evaluated source by source for technical accuracy.
CEO credibility conditional — accepted on business claims, discounted when no independent accountability exists.
Social media rejected entirely as news source — too low signal-to-noise ratio for his information standards.
NGO trust moderate — respects evidence-based advocacy, sceptical of organisations with undisclosed funding.

core_memory.identity_statement: Structural engineer who evaluates institutional trust through the same load-bearing analysis he applies to infrastructure — government fails the test most consistently.

life_defining_events.event: I-35W bridge collapse — professional proximity to system failure at government-engineering intersection
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Government infrastructure decisions permanently rated as high-risk, low-trust

life_defining_events.event: Employee ownership structure design — built shared governance mechanism from inside
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Employer trust highest of any institution, grounded in participatory design

absolute_avoidances: Trust based on authority without accountability mechanism
